sentences.text: --1944 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the liberated city of Kaunas, Kovno, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in contact with Captain Gusev from the border patrol [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the Russian Army.
Basically, the unit which Captain Gusev approached me with was the-- they were called in Russian, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], NKVD.
That is a border patrol unit which is-- during the peacetime, they are the border patrol.
During wartime, they're a department of the NKVD and very respected unit and very effective.
And this is a unit-- regiments-- it's composed from regiments which-- each of the regiment had a number.
Our number was the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Regiment Number 33.
We-- the uniform that we used to wear was green and green hats.
And our mission, basically, was to follow the front line and screen right up to the front line.
After the-- they take over a city, our mission was to find out what kind of resistance-- and everything connected with, with, cleaning up the pockets of resistance.

Everything connected with investigative forces-- investigate whether there is some, some, some sabotage or something that is being planned against the military-- the incoming and liberating military forces.
Before-- as soon I met with Captain Gusev, he explained to me-- he gave me the-- a-- he read me my, my obligations and what actually he plans to do with our group.
And specially-- specifically, he told me that they are going to use me in the beginning, after giving me a certain short-time training in the military because I have never been to the military.
So they have to give me a, a short, extensive training.
And right after that, I'm going to be absorbed within the-- within the-- within this unit, within this regiment.
He also explained to me that-- he didn't give me any specifics where we are going to move.
But ultimately, he told me that our, our mission is going to be to-- towards-- toward Germany, within Germany.
And specifically, he based himself off my knowledge of German.
And he told me also that, in the beginning, we are going to be moved around between Lithuania and Latvia, whereby my Lithuanian came handy.
About-- at that time, about five languages, right.
Now, he was very friendly.
And he gave me-- before recruiting me, he gave me a-- like, about three weeks of time because he had to recruit some other people.
So he gave me some leeway.
And right away, what I did is, I had some relatives who were-- who, who were stationed in Kovno.
And they were homeless, actually.
They didn't have where to stay.
So I, being already a, a, a, a, a future soldier in the-- in the army, I had certain, certain protections and certain powers.
Whereby, I found a, a nice apartment, and I housed them in that apartment.
And plus the fact I told them that-- and, and this apartment was precisely in the vicinity where I used to live before.
It was right in the center of Kaunas.
Time was going very fast.
And in a matter of a few weeks, I was-- I was taken to the-- to, to the army barracks.
They sent me-- in the beginning when they gave me the-- a very expedient and fast training, it was very, very harsh in, in, in a certain way.
But of course, after what we have-- what we have experienced in our life, I mean, this was-- this was nothing.

And I was very proud to be now in the army, whereby I'm going to be participating in a certain way, in a-- in a very minimal way to be able to take some vengeance from those who, who have punished me, and who have done so much harm to myself and to my dear ones.
It was really like a blessing.
Especially-- you're young, and you are looking forward, you know.
And I, I took it very, very easy, you know, the, the, the, the training and the, the kilometers that we had to walk with all the ammunition, and the, the target shooting, and the, the topography.
They gave us all instruction because we're supposed to be future, future officers in the army.

They, they felt that, with the kind of education that we had and the kind of experience-- life experience-- we-- they-- and, and the fast absorbing of all this preparation, they felt that-- the only thing, the only point which I was concerned is whereby in the-- in the next month to come, they wanted very badly I should join the, the Communist Party.
Which-- most of the members of this elite group were members of the party.
And I mentioned to my officer who was-- after, after that-- that was a captain.
But then I had a officer whom I-- I'm suspicious that he was Jewish.
His name was Vinokurov.
And for some reason, I mean, he was very, very, very, extremely friendly to me.
And I-- and I had a, a, a feeling that he was-- he was Jewish, although nobody knew, actually, his whereabouts, yeah.
He was a colonel.
And he always used to treat me very, very specially.
Now, the first mission what we had is in the vicinity of Kaunas.
And then we were transferred to Latvia.

And there we had a-- we were right in a battle where-- between-- because in Latvia, in Courland, was a section where, where there were Vlasov-- see, their Russian, Russian General Vlasov, who had-- he was the Ukrainian who went, actually, in helping the Germans.
And he created the army who was loyal to the Germans and fought on behalf of the Germans.
And they were actually-- part of this army was surrounded in Courland.
And there were going on very, very strong battles.
And one of those, when I was with my officer, which-- I used to translate for him-- we were shelled.
And they have actually shelled our jeep-- fell a, a, a shell and exploded.
And the officer and the, the other guy that was with me-- the only guy that was saved from this happening was myself.
I was-- I lost, actually, my-- I, I was-- lost, actually, my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I was not able to recover.
At least, for-- when I recovered, I find myself in the hospital.
Fortunately, nothing serious happened to me, you know.
And I was back on the-- I lost my consciousness at the time.
And I was back, back at the-- with the-- with the-- with the army.
As time went by, we have-- and then the developments-- military-- went very well for the Russians.
I mean, the, the army was-- just finished liberating most of Lithuania, and Latvia, and also Courland.
And those, those parts-- those pockets of resistance were actually completely eliminated.
Then suddenly, on the-- on the other front with Japan, happened something very strange.

And this was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were also, I forgot-- would like to mention, we were also in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] involved in, in having-- getting agents in every place in order to get all the information pertaining to that-- to that vicinity.
Now, it was in Lithuania-- for the short time that we had taken many of Lithuanians prisoners, especially those-- they used to call them the, the green ones because they used to wear a green uniform.
And they were-- they were the ones that-- they were trying to sabotage the Russians.
And they were the ones that mostly killed, killed the Jews.
And I had, really, a great pleasure in, in the-- investigating some of them and translating.
And, of course, we were not able to kill them at that time because that was a, a, a general order from the High Command.
But those, those people have been-- wound up in prisons.
And I'm sure they were-- they rotted in the prisons in the-- deep in, in Russia.
Then I mentioned to you about the front in-- with, with Japan.

And I found out-- by coincidence I found out, that the whole regiment-- not only ours, there were a few regiment-- ours was one of the regiment that was transferred completely, fully to the Kurilian Islands, which is in the vicinity of Japan.
I was the only one that this colonel gave me a transfer to the front to Germany-- to Zhukov's front.
And this transfer was very, very, very much valid.
And, and it really changed my, my life.
Because all those who went to the Japanese front ultimately wound up in, in Russia-- somewhere in Russia or in Lithuania.
They would have-- they would have never had the opportunity to get out from the Russians.
If, if, if-- for one stroke of the pen, if he would just not have given me the, the, the-- that used to be called in Russian, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN].
That means a transfer.
I remember that I had to change the train.
And I crossed over Warsaw through Poland.

And then I joined in the-- I joined in the-- deep in Germany, in the-- about a hundred-- a few hundred miles from Berlin, I joined the-- a a, a new regiment, which was the 132nd regiment from the-- from the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [RUSSIAN], which is the-- which is the border patrol.

I forgot to mention that before that, we went to Ostpreusen, which is Prussia, you know, in the-- and we had taken the-- liberated the city of Konigsberg, which was called-- later changed to Kaliningrad.
This was before.
This was when we were in Lithuania and in the vicinity of Latvia.
And this was also a remarkable-- a remarkable victory that we had in the-- over the city of-- entering the city of Konigsberg, you know.

It was an incredible experience for us, and especially for those-- the Jews who were in the camps to be able to see the surrender of a large German Army, whom we had to investigate and, and who were falling in our-- in our hands.
But now, in the vicinity of Berlin, we came-- in a matter of a few, few weeks, we were transferred-- we were right-- absorbed-- transferred to the Zhukov Army.
Zhukov was the general who actually liberated Berlin, and who met with Eisenhower at the Elbe at a later date.
So many people don't know about that, but Berlin was actually liberated by a certain segment of the Russian Army.
It was a very fierce battle because the young-- Berlin was surrounded by quite-- by-- quite a number, number of days.
But who fought and who liberated Berlin were the, the, the troops that were-- that were actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mostly prisoners, deserters, rapists, the, the worst of the troops.
They, they, they were assigned to the liberation of Berlin.
And they were assigned specifically-- in order to-- for, I mean, to go to the first lines and go to the battle.
And whoever survived would be commuted his sentence for-- I mean, he would be-- actually, he would be free and, and recognize his rights as a-- again.
So that was a-- it was a very fierce battle.
But it didn't really last too long because Hitler was already exhausted.
And he's-- had the young troops which were not ready to, to fight.
They were too young.
They were-- had, like, 15-, 16-year-old boys who were-- who had a very bad training.
But he held up until the last minute.
And incidentally, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- when we-- when Berlin was liberated, we came in right after-- afterwards.
And incidentally, I had also the privilege to be at the place where Hitler was, was, was burned, at the Reich Chancellery.
And our regiment-- and our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] regiment, actually, was, was the one that had-- was empowered to watch and to be at, at, at this specific, specific place.
And I was there.
I even met some, some American-- at a later date, some American soldiers-- an American, Sergeant [?
Schwartz, ?] which I never knew his first name-- whom we turned over a lot of documents from the Reich Chancellery.
And I want to mention to you that we have seen the corpses, the burned corpses of Eva Braun and of, of Adolf Hitler, which, for-- amazingly, the Russian command took it away.
And, and they were there, laying, for, for a few days.
But then they cleaned it up.
And for some reason, they didn't want divulge anything.
Amazingly, also, that a bomb penetrated the Reich Chancellery, a, a, a-- it's possibly American bomb or a-- or a Russian bomb, and didn't explode, and penetrated.
And during the time that I was there, I was also in charge there in turning over certain documents, which-- the Americans used to come with Studebakers.
And we used to help them load the-- all the information.
Because the Russians were not investigation.
We, we used to investigate only certain, certain, certain parts.
But most of the paperwork, we really didn't have time for that because there was quite a lot to do in, in, in this-- in, in the sector of Berlin.
Also, we have liberated-- which, actually, I myself came in maybe-- I'd been six or seven hours afterward the concentration camp of Sachsenhausen.
And not only this, we had-- we had under our watch-- we took prisoners, the head of the-- the, the head of the camp, who was a captain.
And we took about 16 prisoners, whom we brought in for justice.
And we were going to have a, a trial, a process, parallel almost like the Nuremberg process.
And, and this was in a specific place, which is in, in a section-- in a Berlin section, which was the sector of the-- of the Russian sector.
As you know, where-- they had four sectors, the American, the French, the British-- and, and, and the Russian was in [GERMAN].
And the name of the street was the Ernst Schneller Strasse.
And we were right there on the corner where we had our compound.
And there were-- housed in the prisoners, the 16 main SS prisoners and their head, this captain.
And we investigated them in a-- and prepared them for a process.
But going back to, to the Reich Chancellery, there were many Americans, I mean, American troops who used to come in there and some American visitors who came.
And they used to take little souvenirs from the-- from the walls.
And I looked at them as crazy.
I said, what are they taking, little pieces of tiles or, or walls?
I couldn't understand.
And then I found that there were-- that was souvenir.
Plus the fact that Hitler's bunker underneath of this building was-- the doors were, like, a three-feet width that-- only the, the width of the door, not, not the-- I mean the--
Yeah.
They used to keep them electrically, open and close them.
And now the electricity was out on account of the-- of, of the-- of the bombings.
And to move one of these doors, you need, like, four, four or five people.
And the bunker was like-- it has, like, Beton, which was concrete, maybe 5, 6-- 4 or 5, 6 meters of concrete.
You should have seen that, inside the bunker.
I mean, it was amazing.
I didn't have a, a, a camera to, to shoot pictures, you know.
But I wish I would have had, you know, with a flash.
And it was a whole-- a, a little city within, within, within the-- within the city.
There was all kinds of entrances, all kinds of, of accommodations, you know, which, which was incredible.
I don't know whether now, at this time, it's open to the-- I, I don't think it's open anymore to-- open to the public.
But this, this was an incredible, incredible, experience, you know, to, to be there at that time.
I, I, I changed-- at that time, I, I called myself Arkadi, not Abraham, because they used to make-- tell jokes about Abraham and some-- you know, like, like satire, just very offensive.
You know-- and that the Jews didn't like to fight, that they're always in the-- in the supply departments and that-- they were quite-- it was quite a strong anti-Semitism.
But in, in my case, I had also my-- the colonel that was in charge that I was working with.
His name was Biriuk.
And he had his family.
And I, I was truthfully very close to him.
Because at a later date, we used to have informants.
And most of the informants were extremely wealthy people.
Like, we had a-- one of the informants was the head of the-- of the gramophone factory, you know.
The-- another one was on the slaughterhouse.
I used to come-- I used to have a motorcycle with a side wagon.
And I used to come in.
And they used to load all kinds of goods there.
And I used to take that to Alexanderplatz.
It was a place where we used to sell this.
And I used to bring the, the, the proceeds to, to, to my-- to the commander and give it to him.
Which-- he would give me, in return, some share.
But I used to be very, very straight with him.
And he liked me very much.
Because he used to send packages to Russia.
Were-- most of them were generated by, by, by me.
So he was very friendly.
Plus the fact that he used to go hunting with the head of the-- with the chief of police from Berlin, from East Berlin.
And I used to go with him, hunting birds and, and some other kind of animals, you know.
So I befriended the, the chief of police.
And I asked him-- and I told him that I-- from time to time, I wear also civilian, and the fact that I'm going to sometimes to the-- to the American sector, sometimes to the French sector.
And in order for my protection, I need a, a German Ausweis, a German identification card.
In the beginning, he was a little bit mad.
But then I said, look, I'm in the intelligence.
And truthfully, what-- I mean, it's for my own protection and for the protection of my friends.
Because the word was, you know, whenever we crossed the borderline between the sectors, we were at our own.
I mean, the commander always told me, I mean, if they catch you, I mean, we don't have any, any allegiance with you or anything.
I mean, you are-- you are-- you're on your own.
This was helpful for me later on when, when I planned my escape.
This, this paper was almost the key to my-- to my escape.
I stayed in Berlin from 1945, the-- May 9th, till-- I stayed two years.
And I was elevated to a-- to a lieutenant-- junior lieutenant.
And they wanted me-- still, they wanted very badly I should become a-- join the Communist Party, which I said, I'm, I'm still not ready.
I always tell them that I'm not ready.
I'm working very hard.
I don't have time to prepare myself.
And I just want--
And that's one of the reasons why I was not able to get a, a higher rank.
Because they, they, they look forward to-- I should be a captain, you know.
And I, I just felt very comfortable with it.
And the pay was also very good.
We had a-- we had a double pay.
They used to pay us in, in rubles, and then-- in, in German marks-- in the occupation marks-- and then twice as much in rubles.
So this family that-- I housed them in Kovno, I used to send them packages constantly.
It was that-- they were the only guy-- the only relatives that I had.
Mm-hmm.
And I was able to, to, to send them.
Now, next to our compound, where we had-- and our investigative force was really very, very strong.
I went twice to Nuremberg with, with the colonel.
And to attest that, I have-- the only thing what I was able to, to save is I have a, a, a cigarette box, a plastic, with the inscription of Nuremberg, 1941-1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, I-- we went in as-- just then as, as observers, not, not as participants.

And plus the fact, my-- the, the colonel wanted to get a little bit certain ideas how to conduct the process of the Sachsenhausen crimes, you know, this concentration camp which we were investigating.
It was quite interesting to be there.
And the two times, twice, that-- we were-- we were short.
We were not-- be able able to sit in the process too long.
But I accompanied him.
And it was quite, quite-- experience.
When I came back, I start thinking about my, my demobilization because eventually, they would have demobilized me and sent me back to Lithuania.
And this was a very big concern to me.
And I felt that's not, not my goal.
Plus the fact-- there was something also interesting which I have to mention.
And this is something that I, I feel very rewarded that I was able to, to, in a certain way, to, to get some vengeance, you know.
And this was that the Russian government has decided to transplant some factories-- the IG Farben factory and some other factories-- to take them from Berlin and take them over to Russia.
And the idea was to take the, the whole factory, all the machinery, with the workers, and the scientists, and the engineers, and everybody else.
And it was-- I received a, a, a letter which I, I was-- I was told I cannot open up this letter at a certain time.
It was, like, 21 hours, which was 9 o'clock in the-- in the evening.
And the letter stated that I have, to my disposal, a truck with six soldiers.
And I have to visit-- they gave me a map-- and to visit certain streets and to take in Germans who work for the IG Farben in order to take them.
That should be-- I should start doing that at 12 o'clock midnight.
And by 4 o'clock in the morning, I should be finished with, with, with my task.
And so-- and so did some other officers have done.
And this was really rewarding because I used to come and knock on the door of the German and used to tell them, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And get, get your luggage and your baggage.

And these people were-- I mean, it was something like some satisfaction where, in a certain, little way, I was able to make them feel what I felt, in, in, in a certain way, which was never in the same-- in the same, massive way, you know.
And we have transported-- put them on the railroad station.
Them-- those people, even, even, the people-- the cleaning people because we didn't want to-- the Russian-- the Russian didn't want to leave anybody left, left out.
Everybody had to be transported to, to Russia in order to bring back-- to build back the factory.
And-- because they had the whole-- the equipment, and everything.
Fortunately for those guys, afterwards, I found out that these factories were sent to Leningrad, some of them.
And they wound up somewhere else.
And the Russians were completely disorganized.
And, and till today, I don't know whether these factories were ever brought to fruition.
But at least for me, it was a incredible, incredible experience.
I have to go on with this and, and, and explain to you my escape.
And the--
